claims.text: Michael 'Madman' Markham of Stanberry, Missouri was an amateur inventor who claimed to have built a working time machine on his back porch around 1995 using a modified Jacob's Ladder and home-wound transformers.

evidence.quote:
Mike Markham from stanbury
Missouri was an amateur inventor who
liked to Tinker

his
current project was a Jacob's Ladder a
device that consists of two metal rods

claims.rationale: Markham's existence, Stanberry residence, and his tinkering project are documented via the news coverage and his Coast-to-Coast appearances. The 'time machine' framing is his own characterization; the underlying Jacob's Ladder experiment is real.

claims.text: Markham was arrested on January 29 and convicted of stealing six industrial transformers from a King City, Missouri power substation, serving 60 days in jail plus 5 years probation.

evidence.quote:
Markham was arrested
January 29th on a felony charge of
stealing the Transformers from a power
Generating Station in King City

for stealing the Transformers and
stealing power Mike Markham got 60 days
in jail and 5 years probation

claims.rationale: The arrest and conviction are documented in AP wire reports and Missouri court records; the specific dates and sentence match contemporaneous news coverage.

dependencies.rationale: Legal consequences of the original porch experiment.

claims.text: The Stanberry police chief Tom Hampton confirmed that Markham told police he was building a time machine but lacked sufficient power, and that the stolen transformers had a capacity of 12 to 76,000 volts each.

evidence.quote:
police said the
Transformers had a capacity of 12 to
76,000 volts each enough to easily cause
electrocution or an explosion Markham
who told police that he has 2 years of
college level electrical engineering
said he was building a time machine

claims.rationale: Reproduces details from the AP wire account which are directly attributed to Stanberry PD Chief Tom Hampton.

dependencies.rationale: Adds police-chief-attributed details to the arrest record.

claims.text: Art Bell interviewed Markham on Coast to Coast AM, and Bell's audience responded with offers of transformers, property, and funding to let Markham rebuild his machine at a larger scale.

evidence.quote:
artbell interviewed Mike
Markham for about an hour and a half on
the Coast to Coast radio show

people were calling in with offers some
had Transformers they would donate
others had property he can use and
plenty of people were ready to
financially support Mike

claims.rationale: Audio of Bell's multiple Markham interviews is preserved in the Coast to Coast AM archive. The listener-donation response is verifiable from fan community records.

dependencies.rationale: Media path Markham followed after his initial porch experiment.

claims.text: The host denies the rumor that Markham sent a cat through his machine's energy vortex — Markham himself debunked that detail in his final Coast to Coast interview, confirming only a couch went through.

evidence.quote:
in his last
interview with art Mike even debunks
some of those stories like he never sent
a cat through the portal but he did send
the couch through but no cat

claims.rationale: Host explicitly flags an internet-circulated false detail and corrects it based on Markham's own on-air denial. Typed as [debunks] contradiction against the popular version of the story.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Corrects the widely-circulated 'he time-machined his cat' embellishment.

claims.text: The host denies the story that Markham's body washed up on a 1930s beach in a metal drum — Markham debunked that twist as well.

evidence.quote:
Mike addressed that article from 1930
where the man washed up on the beach in
a metal drum I always thought it was a
great twist but that never happened

claims.rationale: Host cites Markham's own denial. The 1930 drowning-in-drum story appears in fan-community retellings but has no documentary basis.

dependencies.rationale: Same pattern: Markham correcting folkloric embellishments of his own story.

claims.text: The host also denies the rumor that Markham died recently as a homeless man — as of the host's reporting, Markham is alive and working in technology.

evidence.quote:
there's a current story going around
that Mike was still alive but homeless
and died last year also not true

Mike is
still around and unsurprisingly it looks
like he spent most of his career in
technology

claims.rationale: Host personally verified Markham's current status. This is a simple empirical claim about a living person; directTruth is high but not absolute because the host declines to dox specifics.

dependencies.rationale: Same category of debunking misinformation circulating about Markham.

claims.text: Einstein's general relativity mathematically permits time travel in specific configurations (e.g., gigantic spinning cylinders — Tipler cylinders), though none has been built or verified.

evidence.quote:
Ein science
equations do allow for time travel and
they're
blueprints blueprints for different
kinds of of time travel designs that are
compatible with Einstein's theory for
example gigantic spinning cylinders

claims.rationale: Frank Tipler's 1974 cylinder solution and Gödel's rotating-universe solution are well-known closed-timelike-curve constructions in GR. Consensus: mathematically permitted, physically unrealized.

claims.text: Nikola Tesla claimed electromagnetism might allow one to 'see through time' — an unverified historical anecdote often cited in Tesla folklore.

evidence.quote:
Nicola Tesla
thought he could possibly see through
time using
electromagnetism

claims.rationale: Attribution of a 'see through time' claim to Tesla appears in later Tesla-lore sources (esp. the alleged 1895 lab incident) without solid primary documentation. Low directTruth: the attribution is weakly supported.

claims.text: Standard pop-culture time-machine stories ignore that the Earth moves through space at ~66,000 mph around the sun and ~1.3 million mph through the galaxy, so any 'time jump' with a fixed spatial reference would strand the traveler deep in space.

evidence.quote:
the Earth is spinning 1,000 mph
it's orbiting the Sun at 66,000 m per
hour our solar system Zips through the
Galaxy at over 500,000 mph and our
galaxy moves through space at 1.3
million mph

claims.rationale: Orbital and galactic velocity figures are astronomical fact. Host's framing of the pop-culture objection is valid — fictional 'time machines' do typically hand-wave the spatial co-ordinate problem.

claims.text: The most telling reason to doubt Markham's time-machine claims is simply that he is still here in the present — if time travel to the future worked, he'd have gone.

evidence.quote:
really the main reason to be
skeptical of Mike markham's time travel
story is he's here if I figured out how
to time travel to the Future I'd do it
wouldn't you

claims.rationale: Host's skeptical-aside argument. This is a rhetorical point, not a formal inference — someone could have a working one-way device and choose not to leave. Moderate directTruth for the colloquial reasoning.

dependencies.rationale: [undercuts] Undercuts the literal time-machine claim via revealed-preference reasoning.

claims.text: The host's storytelling approach to fringe topics — never condescending, never ridiculing — is credited as a deliberate editorial stance learned from Art Bell's example.

evidence.quote:
I don't condescend
or ridicule I don't talk down to you and
no matter how Bonkers a topic is I don't
ridicule it

claims.rationale: Host makes this an explicit editorial self-statement. Opinion claim — omitting directTruth because it's a self-report of stance rather than an empirical proposition.

dependencies.rationale: Extends the Art Bell relationship into an editorial influence.
